coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots engineered nanofiltration membrane for ultrafast molecular separation
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Ran
Zhang, Runnan
Shen, Jianliang
Liu, Ya-nan
He, Mingrui
You, Xinda
Su, Yanlei
Jiang, Zhongyi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed a new approach to designing and preparing NF membranes through pore engineering by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). An in-situ interfacial polymerization reaction between GQDs and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) took place within the pores of ultrafiltration (UF) membranes, which was followed by thermal treatment. The irreversible shrinkage of membrane bulk material by thermal treatment ensured the robust residence of the GQDs nanoaggregates. The pore structure of the resultant membranes was revealed by scanning electron microscope (SEM), positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS), Brunner–Emmet–Teller (BET) measurements and neutral solutes rejection experiments. The voids among GQDs nanoaggregates formed the pores of resultant membranes, which radius can be tuned in range of 1.21–1.72 nm by the adding amount of GQDs. The resultant membranes exhibited ultrafast water permeation of 244.7 L/(m2 h bar), which was about 5–6 times higher than the reported datas in previous literatures, and the rejection of Alcian blue and Congo red could attain 92.9% and 98.8%, respectively. Long-time operation and chemical exposure further demonstrated the stability of the resultant membranes. The approach reported in this study may open a new avenue for a variety of molecular/ionic separations by reconstructing membrane pore structure through the mediation of nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pore engineering
In-situ interfacial polymerization
Graphene quantum dots
High flux
Chemical stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The process of preparing PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane by using the in-situ interfacial polymerization method. (b) Photograph of pure water and the GQDs aqueous solution with 1.0 wt% GQDs concentration taken under 365 nm UV light. (c) TEM image of GQDs.
Morphologies of the membranes (0#, 5#, 6# membrane and the UF membrane).

(a) Doppler broadening energy spectra of the UF membrane and the 5# membrane. (b) Pore width calculated from the N2 adsorption isotherm using the density functional theory (DFT) for 1# and 5# membranes with different GQDs concentrations. (c) Pore size distributions of 0#, 1#, 2#, and 5#membrane measured by neutral solutes rejection experiments using log-normal fitting method. (d) Schematic diagram of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane structures and separation mechanism.

Properties of the NF membranes prepared at different conditions. (a) Water permeability and separation performances of the 0#, 1#, 2#, 3#, 4# and 5# membrane. (b) Effect of TMC concentration on water flux and rejections of the membranes. (c) Effect of thermal treatment temperature on water flux and rejections of the membranes. All of the above experiments were carried out under 0.5 bar.
The NF performance of the 4# and 5# membranes compared with those of other membranes in literatures (for details see Table S2).
Stability test of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane (2#) in a long-time filtration of 100 ppm Alcian blue. The operation was carried out under 0.5 bar.
The membranes prepared at different conditions.

row.entry: Ratio of mass ratio of GQDs to TMC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Water flux (a) and Alcian blue rejection (b) of 5# membrane before and after treatment. The operating condition was under 0.5 bar.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots engineered nanofiltration membrane for ultrafast molecular separation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, No. 92, Weijin Road, Nankai District, Tianjin 300072, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed a new approach to designing and preparing NF membranes through pore engineering by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). An in-situ interfacial polymerization reaction between GQDs and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) took place within the pores of ultrafiltration (UF) membranes, which was followed by thermal treatment. The irreversible shrinkage of membrane bulk material by thermal treatment ensured the robust residence of the GQDs nanoaggregates. The pore structure of the resultant membranes was revealed by scanning electron microscope (SEM), positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS), Brunner–Emmet–Teller (BET) measurements and neutral solutes rejection experiments. The voids among GQDs nanoaggregates formed the pores of resultant membranes, which radius can be tuned in range of 1.21–1.72 nm by the adding amount of GQDs. The resultant membranes exhibited ultrafast water permeation of 244.7 L/(m2 h bar), which was about 5–6 times higher than the reported datas in previous literatures, and the rejection of Alcian blue and Congo red could attain 92.9% and 98.8%, respectively. Long-time operation and chemical exposure further demonstrated the stability of the resultant membranes. The approach reported in this study may open a new avenue for a variety of molecular/ionic separations by reconstructing membrane pore structure through the mediation of nanomaterials.

An in-situ interfacial polymerization between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in aqueous phase and trimesoyl chloride in organic phase was conducted within the pores of ultrafiltration (UF) membrane, followed by thermal treatment. The pristine UF membrane was transformed into nanofiltration (NF) membrane with superior separation performance and stabilities.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane-based technology has attracted numerous attentions in water separation and purification applications owing to their environmentally-benign and energy-saving merits [1,2]. Usually based on size cut-off property, commercial membranes can implement separation/purification through various aperture ranges [3,4]. Nanofiltration (NF) is a rapidly developing membrane separation technology, which lies in between ultrafiltration (UF) and reverse osmosis (RO). With typical pore size from one to a few nanometers, NF membranes can be able to remove molecular solutes of 1–10 nm in diameter from water [5–7]. Since performance of NF membranes is mainly governed by size and charge exclusion, it has been a long-standing goal to design NF membranes with controllable pore/channel structure and topology that can separate target molecule precisely and transport water rapidly [8,9].

At present, the majority of NF membranes are prepared by constructing a nanoporous active layer onto the supporting layer (often UF membranes) through a facile and versatile interfacial polymerization method [10–12]. Conventional interfacial polymerization is typically formed a polyamide (PA) layer on the UF membranes by polycondensation reaction of a multi-functional amine and an acid chloride. Due to the high solubility of amines, its can diffuse deeply into the organic solvent. A cross-linked active layer with tortuous nanochannels is subsequently covered on the surface of the UF membranes [13]. The pore size of membranes has been reduced, meanwhile, the water permeation is usually hindered by the tortuous nanochannels within the pure PA active layer of NF membranes, resulting in both low permeability and selectivity [14].

In order to improve the permeability and selectivity of NF membranes, several kinds of solid functional nanoparticles were introduced into the active layer for the preparation of the thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes through interfacial polymerization process [11,15,16]. The introduction of nanoparticles created numerous low resistance channels for water transport within active layer through their intrinsic nanopores or interface voids, leading to a significant enhancement in water flux. For example, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) were used to construct the water channels and made a significant impact on the water flux owing to its inherent porosity, well defined pore aperture and channel structure. Wang et al. incorporated COFs into active layer through interfacial polymerization. The water flux of the as-prepared membrane can be increased to 192.5 L/(m2 h Mpa) under the condition that the Na2SO4 rejection rate is higher than 80% [17,18]. GO-modified PA membranes with ultrafast water transport nanochannels from the stacked GO nanosheets were also fabricated. Hu et al. demonstrated that these channels conferred membranes both water permeability and water-salt selectivity properties [19–21]. As one representative, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with smooth hydrophobic graphitic walls and nanoscale inner diameters were also used for the construction of nanochannels within active layer. Liu et al. designed a novel poly(zwitterionic) carbon nanotubes (ZCNT) and embedded them into active layer via interfacial polymerization. With ion-selective channel at surface, the obtained membranes exhibited ion-responsive property and adjustable pore size from 1.24 to 0.54 nm. The resultant membrane can separate mono/multivalent ions while allowing the fast permeation of water [6,22,23]. In summary, great efforts have been dedicated to generate novel active layer with well-organized pore structure and topology on the supporting layer via interfacial polymerization.

Herein, we report a new approach to designing and preparing NF membranes with tunable nanochannels through pore engineering by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) based on porous UF membranes. GQDs was chosen due to its nanoscale size and desired dispersity. As an appealing carbon material, GQDs has aroused general attention in broad application fields because of its quantum size, impressive mechanical, frictionless surface and chemical stability [24]. In our previous work, GQDs with an average particle diameter of ~ 2 nm was prepared and employed to obtain the GQDs/PA TFN membrane with high water flux [25]. In this study, we developed an in-situ interfacial polymerization method for the fabrication of NF membranes. GQDs was chosen as the only water phase monomers which stored within the pores of UF membranes and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) was chosen as the organic phase monomers. The in-situ interfacial polymerization occurred within the pores of UF membrane forming a series of GQDs nanoaggregates. Followed by thermal treatment, the irreversible shrinkage of membrane bulk material ensured the robust residence of the as-formed GQDs nanoaggregates. In this way, the pristine UF membranes were transformed into NF membranes. Positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS), Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET), Scanning electron microscope (SEM), Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were used to reveal the pore structure and surface properties of the resultant NF membranes. Long-time operation and chemical exposure demonstrated the stability of the resultant membranes. The approach reported in this study may open a new avenue to reconstructing the pore structure of membranes through the mediation of nanomaterials for a variety of molecular/ionic separations.

Poly(ether sulfone) (M
w = 29,000 g/mol) was received from BASF Co. (Germany). It was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h to remove excess moisture. N, N-dimethy-formamide (DMF) and ethanol were purchased from Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China) and used as received without other processing. Citric acid (CA) was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). 1,3,5-Benzene-tricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 99 wt%) used as organic phase monomer was purchased from Alfa Co. (USA), while N-heptane used as organic phase solvent was purchased from Yuanli Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Na2SO4, Orange GII (M w = 452), Congo red (M w = 696), Methyl blue (M w = 799) and Alcian blue (M w = 1299) were received from Kewei Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl; 4% aqueous solution) used for chemical stability test were purchased from Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). The DI water in this work was obtained by a reverse osmosis water purifier.

In this work, GQDs were synthesized from citric acid and PES UF membranes were prepared by the non-solvent phase separation (NIPS) method as described elsewhere [25]. The casting solution of PES UF membranes contained PES (16 wt%), PEG (16 wt%) and DMF (68 wt%). The dextran (250 kDa) rejection of the PES UF membranes was 50%.

The 0# membrane was prepared by thermal treatment. After 90 °C treatment, the pore of the PES UF membrane uncontrollable shrank and 0# membrane with a dense surface structure was obtained. For the synthesis of PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes, an in-situ interfacial polymerization reaction between aqueous monomers (GQDs) and organic phase monomers (TMC) was conducted within the pores of the UF membrane. As shown in Table 1, the GQDs solutions with concentration ranged from 0 wt% to 3.5 wt% were prepared in advance. In the first step, the UF membrane was fully immersed in the GQDs aqueous solution and keep for 15 min. After taking out and removing the excess aqueous solution on the membrane surface, the monomer-saturated membrane was immersed in the 0.1 wt% TMC organic solution. In this procedure, GQDs nanoaggregates were formed within the pores of UF membrane via polymerization reaction. 2 min later, the resultant membrane was taken out and thermally treated in an electric oven at 90 °C for 15 min. Finally, the membrane was stored in deionized water for 30 min before subsequent characterization and performance evaluation.

JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to acquire TEM images of the GQDs flakes. The chemical structures of the membranes were recorded on a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 560) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was performed on analyzer (PHI-1600, Perkin-Elmer Co., USA). Morphological changes of the membrane surface and cross-section were inspected by a field-emission scanning electronic microscopy (SEM, Nova Nanosem 430, FEI Co., USA) imaging. The samples were sputter-coated with gold nanoparticles prior to analysis. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope IIIA, USA) measurements in the tapping mode were taken at two locations of each membrane sample and the root mean square (Rms) roughness values were calculated to study the membrane surface topology. The free volume characteristics of the membranes were obtained by the positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS). The energy of the slow positrons emitting from 22Na source and the energy range of position beam was in range of 0.18–20.18 keV. Apparent contact angle was measured by using a contact angle goniometer (JC2000D2M Contact Angle Meter) at room temperature. The membranes surface charge properties were measured via an electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria) with a 1 mmol KCl solution at ambient temperature. Micromeritics Tristar 3000 analyzer was used to explore pore distribution of the PES/GQDs-TMC membrane by nitrogen adsorption at −196 °C, based on the DFT method.

A dead-end filtration cell system was used for the permeation and separation experiments. All the membrane samples were cut into a circle of effective area of 14.3 cm2 and placed in the filtration cell. Prior to each permeation and solute separation experiment, membrane samples were pre-pressurized for 0.5 h at 2 bar using DI water to reach a steady state. After that, the pure water flux J
w1 (L/m2 h) was tested at a low constant operating pressure of 0.5 bar and was calculated as follows:
(1)
J
w
1
=
V
A
∆
T
where V was the volume of permeate pure water (L), A was the effective area of the membrane (m2), ΔT was the time duration of the experiment (h).

The rejection experiments were conducted with single solute dye/salt solutions at a pressure of 0.5 bar. The dye/salt solutions with a concentration of 100 ppm and 1000 ppm respectively were prepared using DI water. The concentrations of the feed and permeation solutions were obtained by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Hitach UV–2800, Hitach Co., Japan) and a conductivity meter (DDS–11A, Shanghai Leichi Instrument Co., Shanghai, China). The solute rejection R (%) was obtained by the following equation:
(2)
R =
C
f
-
C
p
C
f
×100%
where C
p (g/L) and C
f (g/L) was the concentrations of permeate solutions and feed solutions, respectively.

To systematically investigate the pore size and distribution of the membranes, solutes rejection experiments were conducted under 0.5 bar in this work. Dextran solutions (100 ppm) with different molecular weight were used for filtration. In the feed and permeate solution, the concentration of dextran was determined by a chemical oxygen demand analyzer (COD-571, Shanghai Leichi Instrument Co., Shanghai, China).

The membranes used for stability tests were pressurized at 2 bar with DI water (25 °C). The NaOCl (1000 ppm), HCl (pH=1) and NaOH (pH=13) solution was prepared in advance by dissolving the chemical agents in DI water. The membranes used for chemical stability experiments were soaked in different solutions for 12 h. After soaking, the membranes were used for filtration of DI water for 0.5 h to wash away the residual solution. Nanofiltration performance of the PES/GQDs-TMC membranes were measured before and after treatment to evaluate the chemical stability of the membranes. In addition, long-term filtration experiment was carried out by continuous filtration of 100 ppm Alcian blue over 168 h.

GQDs (~ 1.86 nm) synthesized by thermal decomposition of citric acid (CA) was dispersed in aqueous solution through hand shaking ( Fig. 1b-c). GQDs exhibited strong photoluminescent activity because of its isolated sp2 clusters [26]. As shown in Fig. 1b, under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, the homogeneous blue light of GQDs aqueous solution indicated the desirable dispersity of GQDs in water [27].

Fig. 1a showed the fabrication of PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes. When the porous UF membrane was first fully soaked into the GQDs solution, some GQDs flakes with several nanometers in size were entered the pores of the UF membrane in this process and were confined within the pores [19,28]. Generally, the diffusion rate of nanoscale GQDs flakes is slower than that of amines which are commonly used as water monomer in conventional interfacial polymerization [13]. Therefore, as the membrane was immersed in TMC solution, the in-situ interfacial polymerization occurred immediately in the pores of the UF membrane to form a series of GQDs nanoaggregates. Finally, the resultant PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane was treated at 90 °C in an oven. Meanwhile, the GQDs nanoaggregates could be assembled firmly inside tortuous pore channels of the UF membrane. In this way, the pristine UF membranes were transformed into NF membranes. The voids among the GQDs nanoaggregates formed nanochannels of the resultant NF membranes.

To explore the chemical structure of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes, ATR-FTIR measurements were conducted. Compared with 0# membrane, the FTIR spectra of 5# membrane revealed a slightly different peak at 1727 cm−1, verifying the formation of anhydrides and ester groups, which demonstrated the reactions between TMC and GQDs (Fig. S3a, Supporting information). As shown in Fig. 2a, the morphology of the membranes surface was assessed. The UF membrane exhibited a smooth and porous surface. The surface of 0# membrane was denser compared with the UF membrane that no apparent pores could be observed. This phenomenon suggested that thermal treatment led to the densification of polymer matrix, made the polymer chains of the UF membrane pack more tightly [29]. For 5# and 6# membrane, due to the existence of rigid GQDs nanoaggregates in pores of the UF membrane, the pore shrinkage of the membrane bulk material was conducted in a controlled manner. This led to that the surface structure of 5# and 6# membrane was much looser than that of 0# membrane. It is noteworthy that the surface pores of 6# membrane could be seen apparently. We speculated that due to the excessive amount of GQDs (GQDs:TMC = 35:1), it could not be completely reacted with TMC and the leaching of these unreacted GQDs formed large voids within GQDs nanoaggregates. Fig. 2b showed AFM images and surface roughness of the membranes. The UF membrane had Rms roughness value of 15.9 nm whereas 0# membrane had Rms roughness of 22.5 nm, indicating the surface of membrane became much rougher because of the densification after thermal treatment. With the increase of GQDs amount, the shrinkage of membrane bulk material was controlled during thermal treatment and the Rms value of 6# membrane was decreased to 15.8 nm. According to cross section images of the membranes (Fig. 2c), no apparent composite layer on the UF membrane was observed due to the slow diffusion rate of GQDs. The in-situ interfacial polymerization mainly occurred in the pores of the UF membrane. Additionally, the irreversible shrinkage of membrane bulk material by post thermal treatment ensured the robust residence of the GQDs nanoaggregates in the pores. By contrast, few GQDs nanoaggregates which formed on the outer surface of the UF membrane could not exist steadily because of its inflexibility and the weak interactions between the GQDs nanoaggregates and membrane surface. These nanoaggregates would be easily eluted away during subsequent operations. The results preliminarily suggested that the introduction of the GQDs engineered the pore structure of UF membrane by forming nanoaggregates within pores, rather than forming a composite active layer on the UF membrane.

To elucidate the effect of GQDs concentration on the size of nanochannels within PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes, the pore size distribution and free volume characteristics of the membranes were obtained [30]. The free volume properties of the UF membrane and 5# membrane were detected by positron annihilation spectroscopy (PAS) with a fast-slow coincidence system. The S parameter is defined as the ratio of the photon annihilation count in region of 510.2–511.8 keV to the total counts [31]. According to Doppler broadening energy spectroscopy shown in Fig. 3a, the changes of S parameter were correlated to the free volume and structure changes of membranes. Generally speaking, the reduction of S parameters provides information about the decrease of membrane free volume fraction and the increase of the membrane compactness [30,32]. The S value of 5# membrane decreased in comparison with the UF membrane, which indicated that the introduction of GQDs nanoaggregates combined with post thermal treatment could significantly increase the compactness of the UF membrane and endow the membrane with separation performance. Pore width and average diameter distributions of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes (1# and 5#) based on BET analysis were shown in Fig. 3b. For 1# membrane with 0.2 wt% GQDs concentration, a sharp peak in the range of 1.27–2.50 nm and a broader distribution above 10 nm were observed. The sharp peak corresponded to membrane surface with sharp cut-off property, whereas the distribution above 10 nm corresponded to sub-layers of the UF membrane formed by phase separation [33]. The surface pores of 5# membrane were enlarged and mainly in the range of 2.51–3.70 nm. The same trend was obtained by neutral solutes rejection experiments and was shown in Fig. 3c. 0# membrane had small average effective pore diameter of 1.96 nm because of the of dense surface with low porosity after thermal treatment. This result confirmed that the polymer chains packed more closely after thermal treatment, leading to the pronounced morphology changes as depicted in Fig. 2a. For PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes, the voids among the GQDs nanoaggregates formed pores of the NF membranes. The average effective pore diameter of the 1# and 2# membrane with 0.2 wt% and 0.4 wt% GQDs concentration were 2.42 and 2.64 nm. With the increase in GQDs concentration, more GQDs flakes would enter the pores of the UF membrane. The shrinkage of UF membrane bulk material was tuned during thermal treatment and a large number of water channels were introduced. Meanwhile, the shrinkage of membrane bulk material also led to the stabilization of membrane structure, which ensured the robust residence of GQDs nanoaggregates in pores of the UF membrane. When concentration of GQDs reached 1.0 wt%, the size of nanochannels within 5# membrane further increased to 3.44 nm, indicated the existence of large channels formed by the leaching of unreacted GQDs. These results demonstrated that the voids among the GQDs nanoaggregates formed the pores of PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes, which can be tuned from 2.42 nm to 3.44 nm in diameter by the adding amount of GQDs.

The structures of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane was schematically presented in Fig. 3d. On one hand, a large number of GQDs nanoaggregates in pores of the UF membrane could control shrinkage of the UF membrane during thermal treatment and introduce water channels, maintaining the water flux. On the other hand, nanochannels within GQDs nanoaggregates and stack of GQDs flakes could form tunable porous structure, which endowed the membrane with both water permeability and water-salt selectivity properties. To further verify whether there was a dense GQDs-TMC composite layer covered on the surface of the UF membrane, the XPS measurement results of 0# membrane and 5# membrane were obtained (Fig. S7, Supporting information). For the 5# membrane, the existence of S element at 284.6 keV verified there were no GQDs-TMC layer covered on the UF membrane.

The water fluxes and dye/salt rejection of each membrane were tested and the effects of monomer concentrations and thermal treatment temperature were explored in detail. 0# membrane with dense surface and low porosity showed water flux of 7.0 L/(m2 h bar). For 5# membrane, the water flux increased to 244.7 L/(m2 h bar), which was 5–6 times higher than the reported datas in previous literatures (
Fig. 5). According to the structure characterization results, the surface of 0# membrane was denser compared with other membranes and the average effective pore diameter was smaller (1.96 nm). After GQDs pore engineered, a large number of GQDs nanoaggregates in pores of the UF membrane could control shrinkage of the UF membrane during thermal treatment so that the water flux notably increased with the increase in pore diameter (Fig. 4a). The incorporation of GQDs nanoaggregates endowed membrane with loose surface structure, offered additional water pathways enhancing permeate flux. The voids among GQD nanoaggregates with smooth and frictionless surface rendered the water transport channels through the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane and the sizes of nanochannels increased with the increase of GQDs amount [15]. In addition, the surface hydrophilicity of the membranes was investigated (Fig. S5a, Supporting information). Generally, higher affinity toward water represents higher water solubility and higher water flux [34]. The water contact angle continuously decreased from 53.9° of 0# membrane to 41.40° of 5# membrane, owing to the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups from GQDs. Hence, the enhancement in water flux of PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes could be considered as the synergistic effect of suitable pore structures and hydrophilicity of the NF membranes.

As shown in Fig. 4a, the retention rate of all dyes by 0# membrane was higher than 95% due to the size exclusion effect of a dense membrane surface. For the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes (1#, 2#, 3#, 4# and 5#), with the increase of GQDs concentration, the retention of Congo red and Alcian blue was almost unchanged. The rejections of each membrane on Na2SO4 were all below 20.0%. The possible explanations were based on the physical sieving by nanochannels of membranes and the electrostatic repulsive between charged dyes and membrane surface [35]. Physical sieving was the dominant factor in retention of dye molecules. According to Fig. 4a, As GQDs concentration increased from 0.6 to 1.0 wt%, the retention ratios of Orange GII and Methyl blue decreased from 91.2% to 72.6% and 92.9–79.4%, whereas the retention of Alcian blue was remain above 90%. This was consistent with the molecular weight of dyes and the trend of membrane pore size changes. According to the zeta potential of the membranes, all membranes were negatively charged (Fig. S5b, Supporting information). The PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes exhibited higher negative charge compared with 0# membrane and the zeta potential of 5# membrane decreased to −45.91 mV, which was attributed to the abundant carboxyl groups on the edges of GQDs. Considering that Orange GII, Methyl blue and Congo red are all negatively charged, electrostatic repulsive could improve the selectivity of the NF membrane.

The effect of TMC concentration on membrane properties was further demonstrated in this study. As shown in Fig. 4b, the water flux decreased from 244.7 to 149.9 L/(m2 h bar) and the dye rejections increased slightly with the increase of TMC concentration (GQDs concentration fixed at 1.0 wt%). In conventional interfacial polymerization reaction, the rate of polymerization is higher at high TMC concentration, resulting in a denser and compacter composite layer [36]. The result in this study was also verified there was a similar trend in GQDs nanoaggregates and small nanochannels are thus formed. NF performances of membranes treated at different temperatures were evaluated for a fixed treating time of 15 min. Water flux and dye rejection data for each temperature were plotted in Fig. 4c. With the increase of thermal treatment temperature, the water flux of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane decreased and the dye rejection ratios increased. During thermal treatment, the in-situ interfacial polymerization was facilitated to endow the GQDs nanoaggregates with a more cross-linked structure, leading to a decreased water flux and an increased dye rejection [37,38]. In addition, since thermal treatment made the polymer chains pack more tightly and thus reduced the fractional free volume of the polymers, the membrane bulk material had a narrower interstitial chain spacing to assemble GQDs nanoaggregates firmly in the pores and effectively reduced defects [39]. In Fig. 5, we compared the NF performance of the 4# and 5# membranes with those of other membranes in recent literatures. It was worth mentioning that the membrane in this work exhibited superior water flux and comparable dye rejection under relatively lower operation pressure (0.5 bar).

For practical applications, the chemical and mechanical stabilities of the membranes are critical factors [40]. The conventional PA TFC membranes often suffer from chemical-induced degradation during chemical exposure [41]. For example, when PA membrane expose to sodium hypochlorite solutions, chlorine would attack on the amide nitrogens and destroy the PA network [42]
Table 2.

We investigated the effect of chemical exposure on NF performance of the 5# membrane in this study by immersing the membrane for 12 h in several kinds of chemical solutions. For sodium hypochlorite, as the immersion time elapses, the water flux were increased from 109 to 117.5 L/m2 h and the dye rejection of 5# membrane remained almost unchanged, which indicated that the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane had excellent chlorine-resistance performance. The phenomenon was benefited from the existence of GQDs nanoaggregates. The ester groups were much stable than amide linkages in conventional PA TFC membranes during chlorine exposure [43]. HCl (pH=1) and NaOH (pH=13) solution were also used. It was found that the rejection of Alcian blue was decreased from 96.0% to 95.8%, and the water flux was increased from 97.9 to 104.1 L/m2 h after soaking into pH = 1 HCl solution for 12 h. The dye rejection value after soaking into pH = 13 NaOH solution for 12 h was almost unchanged. Additionally, the formation of GQDs nanoaggregates inside the pores bestowed the membrane higher mechanical stability. The dye rejection performance of the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membrane (2#) was very stable during long-term filtration experiment ( Fig. 6), which indicated that the irreversible shrinkage of membrane bulk material by thermal treatment ensured the robust residence of the GQDs nanoaggregates [17,44]. No obvious decrease in the permeation flux was observed over 168 h and the rejection rate for Alcian blue remained constant (97.5%). In summary, the PES/GQDs-TMC NF membranes possessed excellent chemical ability and long-term operation stability, which may facilitate their potential applications in wastewater treatment.

In this study, we report a new approach to designing and preparing NF membranes through the pore engineering by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). In detail, an in-situ interfacial polymerization between nanoscale sized GQDs and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) took place within the pores of UF membrane, which was followed by thermal treatment. The irreversible shrinkage of membrane bulk material by thermal treatment ensured the robust residence of the GQDs nanoaggregates. Nanochannels and voids among the GQDs nanoaggregates endowed the membranes with rapid permeance of water and molecular separation effect. Due to the introduction of GQDs nanoaggregates, the pore structure of the pristine membrane as well as the electronegativity and hydrophilicity was reconstructed. Consequently, with average pore radius from 1.21 nm to 1.72 nm, the water flux of the resultant NF membranes could reach up to 244.7 L/(m2 h bar) and the rejection of Alcian blue and Congo red could attain 92.9% and 98.8%, respectively. Moreover, the resultant membranes possessed superior resistance to chemical reagents due to the chemically robust nature of GQDs and the cross-linking of GQDs and TMC. Long-term operation further demonstrated the stability of the resultant membranes. The approach reported in this study may open a new avenue to reconstructing the pore structure of membranes through the mediation of nanomaterials for a variety of molecular/ionic separations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21621004) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFB0600503).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.memsci.2018.11.044
